coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multicolor luminescent adjustable carbon dots and their application in anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, J.
Lu, W.
Cui, S.
Dong, C.
Shuang, S.
coredata.dc:description: A novel approach for fabricating color-adjustable carbon dots (CDs) is proposed via hydrothermal treatment of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamines, respectively. The as-synthesized CDs can emit blue, green, and orange fluorescence and are named b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, respectively. All of them have strict excitation independence and excellent photostability. Variations of photoluminescence emitting them are attributed to the difference in their particle size, the degree of oxidation, and the content of N-related states on their surface. Furthermore, these multicolor CDs have been used as fluorescents inks, which perform well in anti-counterfeiting and information security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor fluorescence
Fluorescents inks
Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs
Characterization of the b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs
Photoluminescence characteristics of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images, HRTEM images (top right inset) and particle size distributions of (a)+(d) b-CDs, (b)+(e) g-CDs, and (c)+(f) o-CDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS spectra of b-CDs, b-CDs, and o-CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of (a–c) b-CDs, (d–f) g-CDs, and (g–i) o-CDs.
UV−vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of (a) b-CDs, (b) g-CDs, and (c) o-CDs. Excitation−emission matrix for (d) b-CDs, (e)g-CDs, and (f) o-CDs.
Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs.
Possible multicolor photoluminescence mechanism of these CDs.

Anti-counterfeiting application of three CDs. (a) the letters “CARBON”, (b) patterns of dolphin, jellyfish, and chick, and (c) hand-cutting paper in different shapes written or stained with three CDs on filter paper in sunlight and under UV light of 365 nm, respectively.
Information encryption application of three CDs. (a) binary-coded and (b) number code written with three CDs on filter paper in sunlight and under UV light of 365 nm, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the color-tunable CDs and their application in anti-counterfeiting.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multicolor luminescent adjustable carbon dots and their application in anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel approach for fabricating color-adjustable carbon dots (CDs) is proposed via hydrothermal treatment of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamines, respectively. The as-synthesized CDs can emit blue, green, and orange fluorescence and are named b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs, respectively. All of them have strict excitation independence and excellent photostability. Variations of photoluminescence emitting them are attributed to the difference in their particle size, the degree of oxidation, and the content of N-related states on their surface. Furthermore, these multicolor CDs have been used as fluorescents inks, which perform well in anti-counterfeiting and information security.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as the popular star of luminescent nanomaterials, attract significant attention among scientists owing to their fascinating merits including unique photoluminescence (PL), excellent photostability, easy functionalization, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. These intrinsic advantages distinguish CDs from traditional fluorescent materials and enable vast applications in analyte detection [3], bioimaging [4], cancer therapy [5], anti-counterfeiting [6], and light-emitting devices [7]. At present, numerous strategies are developed to fabricate luminescent CDs. Abundant selection of precursors, various synthesis methods and flexible reaction conditions in the synthesis process bring the difference in CDs’ PL behavior. In most cases, the emission of CDs is restricted to the short-wavelength region (blue or green color). Although some CDs emit long-wavelength fluorescence by excitation-dependence characteristic, their corresponding fluorescence quantum yield (QYs) is not ideal (<5%), which limits their practicality in biological imaging, light-emitting devices, information security, etc. [8,9]. Therefore, developing a novel process to fabricate long-wavelength and multicolor CDs under single wavelength excitation, is exceedingly attractive.

Currently, the generally accepted fluorescence mechanisms of CDs include quantum effect, molecular state, surface/edge state and cross-link-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [10,11]. According to these principles, some strategies of color-tunable luminescence CDs have been proposed. One effective method to obtain multicolor CDs is separation of particle sizes, where large sizes enhance the π-conjugated degree and reduce the energy gap of the system, leading to a redshift of emission [12,13]. For example, Kang et al. [14] collected full-color emitting CDs with different diameters by silica column chromatography. In another way, the luminescence color of CDs is sensitive to the polarity of solvent, which may be attributed to solvent dipoles and hydrogen bonding changing the surface state of CDs [15,16]. Yu et al. [17] prepared solvent-dependent CDs and their emission colors vary from green, yellow, orange to red with the increase of polarity of CCl4, toluene, CHCl3, acetone, DMF, CH3OH, and H2O. In addition, precursor screening and heteroatoms doping are useful methods to modify their surface state [18,19]. The precursor containing an aromatic ring can easily form a large conjugated structure during carbonization and condensation process, thus increasing the probability of long-wavelength emission [20,21]. And the participation of heteroatoms such as N, S, and P can adjust energy gap and electronic structures of CDs as well as change optical characteristics [22,23]. Jiang et al. [24] used phenylenediamines isomers (o-PD, m-PD and p-PD) as raw materials and self-dopant to preparate yellow, green, and red fluorescence CDs and ascribed the PL redshift to particle size and the nitrogen content of these CDs. This work opens a new door for controllable preparation of color-tunable fluorescent CDs. Gong et al. [12] introduced another aromatic ring precursor (4-aminobenzoic acid) into phenylenediamine, to develop green, yellow, and orange fluorescent CDs, and claimed that the redshift was closely associated with the degree of oxidation and amidation of surface state. However, the above reports focus on single-element doping. Comparing with single-element doping, dual-doping can rely on the synergistic effect to bring a rich electronic structure of CDs, create diversity of emission state, and improve the probability of color-tunable PL [25,26]. But there are few such attempts. Thus, this work tries to use the precursor containing multiple aromatic ring and heteroatoms to fabricate long-wavelength and multicolor CDs and explore their PL mechanism and multifunctional application.

Herein, we select p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamine as precursors to synthesize color-tunable fluorescent CDs. The resulting CDs possess different optical properties. Variations of PL emission of them are originated from the difference in their particle size, the degree of oxidation, and the amounts of N-related states on their surface. Furthermore, three CDs aqueous are successfully utilized as fluorescence inks for advanced anti-counterfeiting (Scheme 1 ).

p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (p-ABSA), o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamine (PD) are bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. The reagents reach analytical grade and can be directly used. Deionized (DI) water (≥18.25MU·cm) is used in all the experiment procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor CDs (b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs) were fabricated by hydrothermal reaction with p-ABSA and x-PD (x = o, m, p) as precursors. In brief, 173.19 mg of p-ABSA and 108.14 mg of x-PD (x = o, m, p) were first dissolved into 10 mL of DI water and mixed uniformity. Then, the mixtures were sealed in stainless-steel autoclaves and reacted for 5 h under 200 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained suspensions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to discard the impurities and sediment. And the collected supernatants were dialyzed through dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) for 12 h. The powders of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We use TEM to characterize the morphology and size of three CDs. These CDs are spherical and evenly dispersed. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1 a–c) show the lattice spacing of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs are 0.21, 0.22, and 0.22 nm, respectively, which are in line with the facet (100) of graphene. The average diameter is 3.86 nm for b-CDs, 4.59 nm for g-CDs, and 5.19 nm for o-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1d–f). FTIR and XPS spectra are employed to research their surface composition. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, these CDs show similar FTIR spectra in the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H (3200-3500 cm−1), C–H (2810-3043 cm−1), S–H (2550-2690 cm−1), CO (1520-1683 cm−1), C–N (1496 cm−1), CC (1280-1450 cm−1) and C–O/SO (1093-1210 cm−1), respectively. The absorption peaks of CO (1599 cm−1) and C–N (1496 cm−1) with different intensities indicate three CDs have different percentages of bonds. The XPS spectra of three CDs all show four obvious peaks of C1s (285.7 eV), N1s (401.3 eV), O1s (528.5 eV), and S2p (169.1 eV) (Fig. 2b). The intensities of O1s and N1s gradually increase with the redshift of PL emission. As shown in Fig. 3 a, d and g, the C1s peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV and 286.6 eV belong to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O/C–S, and CO, respectively. The ratio of CO increases gradually from b-CDs to o-CDs. The CO group can produce local isolation and cause strain and deformation of the carbon base plane, thus narrowing the energy gap and eventually leading to PL redshift [27]. The high-resolved N1s spectra are divided into three peaks, which correspond to pyridinic N (399.3 eV), amino N (401.3 eV), and pyrrolic N (401.9 eV). With the redshift of emission, the proportion of pyrrole N increases significantly while that of amino N decreases (Fig. 3b, e and h). The O1s exhibit three main peaks, corresponding to S–O/SO (531.2 eV), C–O (531.9 eV), and CO (533.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 3c, f and i). The S2p peaks at 168.3 eV, 169.1 eV, and 169.9 eV indicate the presence of S2P3/2, S2P½, and S–O/SO, respectively (Fig. S1). The above results imply that these CDs are composed of tight carbon cores and multifunctional modified surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence characteristics of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We use UV-vis absorption and PL spectra to study the PL characters of three CDs. Fig. 4 a–c show that the absorption peaks at 310 nm (b-CDs), 344 nm (g-CDs), and 272 nm (o-CDs) are originated from the π-π∗ transition of the sp2 aromatic [28,29]. The broad peaks at 440 nm (g-CDs) and 518 nm (o-CDs) correspond to the n-π∗ transition of the surface-attached functional groups (e.g., CO, C–N or SO) [30,31]. The optimum excitation and emission peaks of three CDs are located at 300 nm/360 nm, 440 nm/530 nm, and 530 nm/600 nm, accompanied by bright blue, green, and orange emitting under 365 nm UV light, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 4d–f, these CDs exhibit excitation-independent characteristics, indicating the presence of concentrated size distribution and uniform emission state in three CDs. We further use CIE coordinates to characteristic the PL properties of b-CDs (0.1578, 0.0705), g-CDs (0.2990, 0.6047), and o-CDs (0.5846, 0.4146) in Fig. 5 , which demonstrate these CDs hold potential for multicolor fluorescence anti-counterfeiting. Furthermore, the relative QYs of three CDs are calculated to be 27.3%, 18.4%, and 16.2%, respectively (Fig. S2 and Tables S1–3). And the fluorescence lifetimes of them are measured to be approximately 3.95 ns, 5.60 ns, and 2.29 ns (Fig. S3), respectively.

To evaluate the photostability of three CDs, we measured their Zeta-potentials. The values are 2.58 mV of b-CDs, 14.80 mV of g-CDs, and 20.64 mV of o-CDs, respectively. The strong electrostatic repulsion makes them have excellent stability in aqueous solutions. Then we studied the photostability of three CDs under different pH, high ion concentration, and 500 W xenon lamp irradiation (Fig. S4). When the pH increases from 3 to 12, the hydroxide ions in PBS solution will neutralize surface charge by deprotonation and subsequently the π-π∗ or n-π∗ transition in three CDs, resulting in fluorescence intensity loss [32]. So, the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs show decreasing trend with the increase of pH (Fig. S4a and Table S4). Fig. S4b and Table S5 depict that the PL intensity of three CDs shows good patience even the concentration of NaCl up to 1.0 mM. Additionally, Fig. S4c shows that the PL intensity decreases mildly after continuous xenon arc light irradiation (500 W) for 180 min. These results prove that b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs have excellent optical stability and can act as good inks for anti-counterfeiting and information security.

We prepared blue, green, and orange CDs via hydrothermal treatment of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamines, respectively. In the carbonization process at high temperature, there are differences in binding site, cyclization structure, and cross-linking degree between different phenylenediamines and p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, resulting in diversity in the carbon cores and surface states of three CDs. Through TEM characterization, we find that three CDs are spherical nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 nm and have graphite-like lattice structures. With the increase of particle sizes from b-CDs to o-CDs, the emission color of these CDs changes from blue to green and orange, because large dimensions enhance π-conjugated degree and reduce the energy gap, thereby the emission redshift [33,34]. FTIR and XPS results clearly demonstrate that three CDs have a similar chemical composition and surface functional groups. From b-CDs to o-CDs, the proportion of O1s, N1s, CO, and pyrrolic N gradually increase, indicating higher degree of oxidation and more N-related states on CDs surface occurred. The increase of surface oxidation degree and N-related states can form more surface defects to capture centers for excitons, which narrow energy gaps and promote emission redshift [13,27,35]. In addition, we study the energy gap of three CDs by cyclic voltammetry to further explain the change of PL. The quasi-reversible oxidation (Eox) and reduction (Ered) peaks of them in Fig. S5 are located at 0.58 V and −0.45 V of b-CDs, 0.35 V and −0.10 V of g-CDs, 0.36 V and 0.02 V of o-CDs, respectively. And the values of EHOMO, ELUMO, and energy gap (ΔE) can be calculated by the following formula:
(1)
E HOMO
= − e
( Eox + 4.4 )
(2)
E LUMO
= − e
( Ered + 4.4 )
(3)
Δ E =
E LUMO
−
E HOMO

The (EHOMO, ELUMO) values of b-CDs, g-CDs, and o-CDs are (−4.98 eV, −3.95 eV), (−4.75 eV, −4.3 eV), and (−4.76 eV, −4.42 eV), respectively. As the redshift of PL emission, the energy gap gradually decreases from 1.03 eV to 0.34 eV, which confirms our conjecture. As a result, we believe that the different PL behavior of these CDs is caused by the synergistic effects of particle size, the degree of oxidation, and the content of N-related states on their surface (Fig. 6 ).

The excellent photostability and multicolor emissive properties of three CDs make their solution to be effective security inks for fast and smart anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. In Fig. 7 a, the letters handwritten with three CDs' inks are nearly invisible under the sunlight, while the letters “CARBON” can emit multicolor fluorescence when the 365 nm UV lamp turns on. Likewise, upon 365 nm irradiation, the patterns of dolphin, jellyfish, and chick exhibit bright blue, green, and orange fluorescence, respectively. Once the excitation is turned off, these patterns cannot be easily recognized by the naked eye under daylight lamp (Fig. 7b). In addition, we hand-made different shapes of paper-cutting and soaked them with three CDs’ inks for 48 h. After drying, different kinds of paper-cuttings can be seen with a slight color attachment in daylight and display strong color fluctuation from blue to orange under 365 nm UV (Fig. 7c).

We further explore their potential value in information encryption. For example, we design a microarray data storage pattern based on the standard 8-bit ASCII character binary code, using b-CDs ink and g-CDs ink spots refer “0” and “1”. As shown in Fig. 8 a, all spots are nearly colorless under daylight, making it impossible for the naked eye to read valid information. After switching on the UV lamp, the spots marked with b-CDs ink and g-CDs ink show bright blue and green fluorescence. And the hidden code can be identified and decoded into “01010011,” “01011000,” and “01010101,” which corresponds to capital letters “S” “X” and “U” on the basis of ASCII binary code. Moreover, the security information (e.g., the code “2022”) was written on filter paper with three CDs’ ink. The security information only can be identified under FL mode (Fig. 8b). Evidently, the above results indicated that three CDs can be potentially used in high-level anti-counterfeiting and protecting important information.

In summary, a color-tunable way has been proposed to preparate blue, green, and orange CDs. Alterations of fluorescence behavior of three CDs are attributed to the difference in their particle size, the degree of oxidation, and the amounts of N-related states on their surface. Moreover, three CDs are extended to anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Other potential applications of three CDs, such as in sensing and bioimaging, are still undergoing in our lab.

Jing Jia: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing-Original draft preparation, Writing-Review & Editing; Wenjing Lu: Project administration, Supervision; Sai Cui: Investigation; Chuan Dong: Supervision; Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and 91843301), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201901D211155).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.100972.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative structure–activity relationship enables the rational design of lipid droplet-targeting carbon dots for visualizing bisphenol A-induced nonalcoholic fatty liver disease-like changes

Li, Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship Enables the Rational Design of Lipid Droplet-Targeting Carbon Dots for Visualizing Bisphenol A-Induced Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease-like Changes, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 13(37) (2021) 44086-44095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor tunable highly luminescent carbon dots for remote force measurement and white light emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, M. Zhang, Y. Wu, R. Zhang, Y. Cao, X. Xu, X. Chen, L. Cai, Q. Xu, Multicolor tunable highly luminescent carbon dots for remote force measurement and white light emitting diodes, Chem. Commun. 55(81) (2019) 12164-12167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of far-red to near-infrared emitting carbon dots for ultrafast lysosomal polarity imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, H. Qin, X. Geng, R. Yang, L. Qu, A.N. Kani, Z. Li, Rational design of far-red to near-infrared emitting carbon dots for ultrafast lysosomal polarity imaging, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 12(28) (2020) 31738-31744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 127(18) (2015) 5450-5453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrahigh efficient FRET ratiometric fluorescence biosensor for visual detection of alkaline phosphatase activity and its inhibitor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhan, S. Yang, L. Chen, Y. Zeng, L. Li, Z. Lin, L. Guo, W. Xu, Ultrahigh Efficient FRET Ratiometric Fluorescence Biosensor for Visual Detection of Alkaline Phosphatase Activity and Its Inhibitor, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 9(38) (2021) 12922-12929.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amplified fluorescence detection and adsorption of Au3+ by the fluorescent melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Qin, X. Yu, L. Xu, Amplified fluorescence detection and adsorption of Au3+ by the fluorescent melamine formaldehyde microspheres incorporated with N and S co-doped carbon dots, J. Hazard. Mater. 405 (2021) 123978-123987.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, R. Wang, B. Wang, Z. Deng, Y. Jin, Y. Kang, J. Chen, Orange, yellow and blue luminescent carbon dots controlled by surface state for multicolor cellular imaging, light emission and illumination, Microchimica Acta 185 (2018) 539-546.

Chem. 91(22) (2019) 14681-14690.
